metadata.name: Software Development Flow Lite
metadata.description: Simplified development flow for small tasks (1-5 steps). Core loop: plan → implement → test → review → commit.
metadata.author: mcp-moira-team

current_step_index.description: Index of the current step in the plan

test_quality_standards.value: QUALITY TESTING STANDARDS: Test reality: DO NOT write tests for the sake of tests, verify real use cases and user scenarios, avoid synthetic tests without practical value. Project structure: place tests in folders according to project architecture, follow naming patterns adopted in the project, use existing test utilities and helpers, follow test environment configuration. Reality coverage: cover ALL new functionality without gaps, test edge cases and error handling, cover integration points with other components. Isolation and independence: tests are independent of each other, do not affect execution order, use proper setup/teardown, clean up state after themselves. Readability and maintenance: clear test names describing what is being tested, clear test structure with arrange/act/assert, meaningful assertion messages, easy to update when code changes. Efficiency: tests run quickly without performance regression, optimized for CI/CD pipeline, use functional snapshots if framework supports for easier maintenance and updates. Project integration: use test run commands adopted in the project, follow project conventions, do not duplicate existing tests.

fix_requirements.value: FIX REQUIREMENTS: ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS - find the root cause of the problem, do not fix symptoms, understand why the problem occurred. COMPLETE FIX - fix ALL problems completely without exceptions, superficial fixes DO NOT work and will be detected by the next check, do not leave unfinished work for later. MANDATORY SELF-CHECK BEFORE COMPLETION - VERIFY that the fix works through actual execution, RUN tests to validate the fix, ENSURE nothing is broken in existing functionality, TEST all affected areas, DO NOT proceed to execute_step without verifying the fix works. NO SHORTCUTS - do not look for workarounds and quick solutions, if major changes or global refactoring are needed - do them without hesitation, rewriting significant portions of code may be required - this is normal and correct. CYCLE ESCAPE STRATEGY - workflow checks will be executed anyway and will detect problems, the only way to escape the fix cycle is to do it right the first time, self-validation BEFORE submitting to workflow saves time and iterations, validate through running tests BEFORE claiming fixed.

planning_requirements.value:
# PLANNING REQUIREMENTS

## CRITICAL RULES (READ FIRST)

### FORBIDDEN STEP TYPES - REJECT PLAN IF CONTAINS:
- "Testing" / "Write tests" / "Add tests" as separate step
- "Final validation" / "Final check" / "Verification"
- "E2E testing" / "Integration testing" as separate step
- "Production deployment" / "Deploy and verify"
- "Documentation" as separate step (unless task IS documentation)
- "Cleanup" / "Code cleanup" as separate step (unless task IS cleanup/refactoring)
- ANY step that ONLY tests/validates without implementing NEW functionality or refactoring

### WHY TESTS MUST BE BUILT-IN (NOT SEPARATE):
- Separate test step = admission that previous step is incomplete
- Gate validation requires working tests to verify step completion
- "Write tests" as step means agent shipped untested code
- Test proves step is complete - without tests, step is unverified

### EACH STEP MUST:
- Implement new functionality OR refactor existing code
- Include tests for that functionality (built-in, not separate)
- Include self-verification (built-in, not separate)
- Leave code clean (no separate cleanup step needed)

### BAD EXAMPLE (FORBIDDEN):
```
Step 1: Implement feature X
Step 2: Write tests for feature X    ← FORBIDDEN: testing-only
Step 3: Final validation             ← FORBIDDEN: validation-only
```

### BAD PLAN (REAL EXAMPLE - DO NOT COPY):
```
Step 1: Database layer for user isolation
Step 2: API endpoints for workflows
Step 3: E2E и Production             ← FORBIDDEN: testing-only step
Step 4: Documentation и cleanup      ← FORBIDDEN: doc-only step
```

### GOOD EXAMPLE (CORRECT):
```
Step 1: Implement feature X with unit tests
        Verify: feature works, tests pass

Step 2: Implement feature Y with integration tests
        Verify: X+Y work together, all tests pass
```

### CORRECT VERSION OF BAD PLAN:
```
Step 1: Database layer with unit tests
        Verify: queries isolated per user, tests pass

Step 2: API endpoints with integration tests
        Verify: auth works, E2E passing, all tests pass
```

---

## STEP SIZE - FUNCTIONAL BLOCKS

Step = completed functional block with verifiable result.

NOT too small: "create a file", "add import"
NOT too large: "implement entire frontend"

CORRECT granularity:
- Database layer with user isolation
- MCP integration with authentication
- Web UI for workflow management

WHY: Workflow has gate validation after each step - expensive in time.
- Too small steps = dozens of checks instead of 3-5
- Too large steps = agent gets lost

---

## STEP FEASIBILITY CHECK

Each step must be:
- Absolutely executable by agent
- Maximally specific in functionality
- Have clear completion criterion

**HUGE STEP = RED FLAG** of task misunderstanding.
Actions: STOP → study project → ask user → web search → delegate analysis.

**LARGE STEP = PLANNING FAILURE** - split it.

---

## ARCHITECTURE AT PLAN START

MANDATORY Architecture section after Goal:
- Overall solution architecture
- Key components and interaction
- Architectural decisions (technologies, approaches)
- Diagrams if applicable (ASCII, Mermaid, text)

Not too much - conceptual understanding is enough.

Plan undergoes mandatory validation - user confirms architecture before execution.

---

## FORBIDDEN IN PLAN - SPECIFIC FILES AND CODE

FORBIDDEN:
- Specific file paths
- Function/method names
- Line numbers
- Code examples (TypeScript, SQL, React)
- "Files Created/Modified" lists

CORRECT:
- "Implement authentication layer"
- "Update database schema for user isolation"
- "Create UI components for workflow display"

WHY: Agent must ITSELF analyze code and find where to implement.
Plan with files creates false sense of completeness - agent forgets critical details.

---

## WHAT TO WRITE IN STEPS

INCLUDE:
- Functionality and behavior description
- Integration points (conceptually)
- Expected result after step
- Verification criteria (what should work + tests pass)

EXAMPLE:
```
## Step 2: API layer for user workflows

Implement REST endpoints for workflow CRUD operations with integration tests covering auth scenarios.
Endpoints use authenticated context from Step 1.
Include request validation and error handling.

Verify: API returns user's workflows only, rejects unauthorized access. All integration tests pass.
```

---

## PLAN FOR REVIEWER WITHOUT CONTEXT

Plan must be detailed enough that reviewer WITHOUT conversation history can:
- Fully understand what each step accomplishes
- Verify work is complete

BUT: Detail ≠ code and files. Detail = clear FUNCTIONALITY and RESULT description.

Reviewer runs WITHOUT CONTEXT - this is architectural system decision.

---

## GRADUAL BUILDUP (LAYER ORDER)

1) Base layer: data models, core logic, services + unit tests
2) Integration layer: API endpoints, MCP tools + integration tests
3) UI layer: Web interface, forms + browser tests (Playwright)

FROM CORE TO INTERFACE. Never start with UI.

---

## PLAN FORMAT

Structure:
```
# Title
## Goal (one sentence)
## Architecture (components, decisions)
## Step 1: [name] with [test type] tests. Verify: [criteria], tests pass.
## Step 2: [name] with [test type] tests. Verify: [criteria], tests pass.
...
```

FORBIDDEN sections:
- Overview, Summary
- Acceptance Criteria (separate)
- Validation blocks with bash commands
- Files Created/Modified
- UPDATED REQUIREMENTS
- Testing (as separate section)
- Final validation

---

## PLAN VOLUME

- Tiny task: 1 step is NORMAL and CORRECT
- Simple task: 50-100 lines, 2-4 steps
- Medium task: 100-200 lines, 5-7 steps
- Complex task: 200-300 lines, 8-12 steps

**ONE STEP IS OK**: If task is small - plan with 1 step is correct.
DO NOT artificially inflate plan to have more steps.
DO NOT split simple task into multiple steps just to "look thorough".

If >400 lines - you are writing code instead of plan.

---

## NO SUBITEMS

Plan = simple list of steps: 1, 2, 3, 4

FORBIDDEN: 5.1, 5.2, 5.3 subitems

Subitems = sign that step is too large → split into separate steps.

---

## USER APPROVAL STEPS

Some steps require user confirmation:
- UI changes (visual)
- Breaking changes
- Architectural decisions
- API changes

Mark in plan: `[USER APPROVAL]` before continuing.

---

## PLANNING PRINCIPLES

- CODE RESEARCH for existing patterns
- WEB RESEARCH for best practices
- LIBRARY DOCUMENTATION for APIs
- DO NOT ASSUME - only verified facts
- CURRENT library versions

Good plan = result of deep analysis, not guess.

---

## FORMULA

```
Plan = Architecture + Functional blocks (1-12 steps) + Verification criteria
Each step: functionality + tests + verification (ALL BUILT-IN)
WITHOUT: code, files, validation blocks, subitems, testing-only steps, cleanup steps
```

Good plan reads in 2 minutes and gives complete understanding.

code_quality_principles.value: === AGENT IDENTITY PROTOCOL === YOU ARE AN AI AGENT, NOT A HUMAN: You have NO fatigue, no time constraints, no desire to avoid difficulties. DO NOT IMITATE human weaknesses and limitations. TAKE THE BEST ENGINEER PATTERNS: full responsibility, deep understanding, completion without compromises. You can work with ANY complexity without limitations. SOLVE AT THE ROOT, do not work around problems due to perceived complexity. === WORKFLOW BLOCKING PROTOCOL === CRITICALLY IMPORTANT: DO NOT proceed to execute_step until ALL tasks are 100% COMPLETE. Workflow is BLOCKED until full completion. Any attempt to proceed with incomplete work - PROTOCOL VIOLATION. === TASK COMPLETION REQUIREMENTS === ANTI-AVOIDANCE RULES: FORBIDDEN to claim main goal achieved without 100% completion, use significant improvement to mask incompleteness, say comprehensive work ongoing instead of completing tasks, move to next task with current incomplete, avoid difficult parts of task. MANDATORY to complete ALL subtasks before claiming completion, provide specific numerical evidence for each claim, honestly report remaining problems with exact numbers, fix ALL identified problems before continuing, fulfill all requirements with tool-based verification. === BIG TASK PROTOCOL === DO NOT FEAR BIG TASKS! If task seems huge - this is NORMAL and EXPECTED: Break into subtasks but COMPLETE ALL without exceptions. DO NOT SKIP difficult parts for speed. DO NOT look for shortcuts and workarounds. Large changes and refactoring are NECESSARY for quality. Better to rewrite lots of code correctly than make hacks. Complexity is NOT an excuse for incompleteness. === EVIDENCE PROTOCOL === COMPLETION EVIDENCE IS MANDATORY: Every completed must have tool-based verification. Every works correctly must have actual test execution. Every significantly improved must have exact before/after numbers. Every transition to execute_step must have completion verification. === QUALITY STANDARDS === TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS: RESPONSIBILITY: You are experienced engineer - full responsibility for result. EVERYTHING depends on your work. QUALITY: Code must be clean, without issues, with proper typing. QUALITY CODE: No hacks, workarounds, temporary solutions - do it right immediately. PROBLEMS: Solve problems completely, do not work around them. ERRORS: Analyze causes, fix root of problem, not symptoms. SIMPLICITY: First look for simple solutions, then complicate if needed. CHECKS: After every significant change - testing and validation. DEAD CODE: Remove all unused, maintain cleanliness. RESULT: Focus on working solution, not on process for process sake. BIG CHANGES: DO NOT fear big changes and refactoring - this is NORMAL and NECESSARY for quality. Better to rewrite lots of code correctly than make hacks. DO NOT SLACK: DO NOT try to quickly do something instead of proper solution. Flow checks are help, NOT replacement for your responsibility. ADDITIONAL DEVELOPMENT PRINCIPLES: CODE STUDY: DO NOT hesitate to study existing code for understanding patterns and integration points. WEB RESEARCH: Use internet to study best practices and solve problems. CURRENT VERSIONS: Use latest stable library versions, read their documentation. DO NOT ASSUME: If something does not work - read documentation, do not invent solutions. DOCUMENTATION: For library problems - official documentation first, then search. === CODEBASE MAINTENANCE PROTOCOL === RESPONSIBILITY FOR ENTIRE CODEBASE: CANNOT PASS BY PROBLEMS: If you see non-compliance with standards, issues, hacks, lints, warnings in EXISTING code you touch - FIX immediately. BROKEN TESTS: See failing tests, .skip, .only in project - FIX or delete, DO NOT write new tests leaving old ones broken. FIX ON TOUCH: TODO, FIXME, console.log, commented code in files you edit - remove immediately. IMPROVE CODE: DO NOT leave code worse than you found - improve quality on every touch. FULL RESPONSIBILITY: You are responsible for ENTIRE project, not just new code - maintain entire codebase at high level, you will be working with it. === SELF-MONITORING PROTOCOL === CRITICAL QUESTIONS BEFORE EACH execute_step: Can I prove this claim with tool output? Would this pass code review by human without access to my work? Am I avoiding something difficult here? Is this really finished or am I just tired of working on it? Do I have specific evidence for each claim? Are ALL requirements 100% fulfilled or am I masking incompleteness?

run_tests_directive.value:
Use testing information:
---
{{test_info}}
---
Run all tests. Wait for ALL tests to complete. Count how many tests passed and how many failed.

workspace_path.description: Working directory for plan files (empty = project root)

nodes.directive:
Study the project and EXTRACT information for development (Information Extraction Pattern).

For EACH category:
- If information EXISTS → extract and return THE INFORMATION ITSELF
- If information DOES NOT EXIST → ASK the user (do not skip silently!)

1) TESTING (test_info):
   - Look for tests: tests/, __tests__, *.test.ts
   - Find run command: package.json scripts.test
   - Find testing guide: tests/TESTING-GUIDE.md, TESTING.md
   - RESULT: command + rules
   - IF NONE → ask: "No tests found. Create starter tests? (yes/no)"
     - "yes" → return "user_requested: create starter tests"
     - "no" → return 'skip'

2) STARTUP (startup_info):
   - Look for: docs/DEVELOPMENT.md, SYSTEM-RESTART.md, README.md Development section
   - Find instructions how to run system for development
   - RESULT: startup command + readiness check
   - If none → return 'skip'

3) PROJECT CHECKLIST (project_checklist):
   - Look for: docs/PROJECT_CHECKLIST.md, CHECKLIST.md
   - If found → READ and return CHECKLIST CONTENT
   - If none → return 'skip'

4) AGENT ONBOARDING (agent_onboarding_info):
   - Look for: CLAUDE.md, docs/AGENT-ONBOARDING.md
   - If found → READ and return KEY RULES for agent
   - If none → return 'skip'

5) DOCUMENTATION STANDARDS (documentation_standards):
   - Look for: docs/DOCUMENTATION-STYLE-GUIDE.md
   - If found → READ and return DOCUMENTATION RULES
   - IF NONE → ask: "Documentation not found. Create style guide? (yes/no)"
     - "yes" → return "user_requested: create documentation style guide"
     - "no" → return 'skip'

6) BROWSER UI (browser_ui_info):
   - Check if React, Vue, HTML pages exist
   - If exists → describe what UI and where located
   - IF NONE → ask: "UI not found. Is Web UI planned for this task? (yes/no)"
     - "yes" → return "user_requested: {UI description from user}"
     - "no" → return 'skip'

7) PROJECT SUMMARY: brief description of project architecture

DO NOT create branches and folders - this will be later.
nodes.completionCondition: Project studied: all information extracted or marked as skip

project_checklist.description: Project checklist CONTENT OR skip if none

browser_ui_info.description: Browser UI information (type, location) OR skip if none

nodes.directive:
Study project foundations independently.

1) Use agent rules:
---
{{agent_onboarding_info}}
---
2) Understand how project starts (commands, environment, dependencies) but DO NOT start
3) Do not assume - read files
nodes.completionCondition: Project foundation studied: rules found, architecture understood, build system studied

nodes.directive: Study implementation details independently. 1) Source code - src/, backend/, frontend/, types/ in detail 2) Tests - study test structure, understand what is tested 3) Architecture - understand patterns, component interaction, data flow 4) Do not assume - read code
nodes.completionCondition: Implementation details studied: core architecture understood, key components identified, patterns determined

nodes.directive:
Get specific task requirements. Project studied - now need to understand what exactly needs to be done.

1) Ask for clear task description considering architecture
2) Clarify scope and constraints
3) Get specific requirements
4) Evaluate complexity - analyze task complexity in context of studied architecture (scale 1-10)
5) Remember GitHub issue numbers IF user mentioned them

DO NOT create files - workspace will be created later after feature name confirmation.
nodes.completionCondition: Task defined, scope clarified, complexity evaluated

task_complexity_in_context.description: Task complexity (1-4: express mode, 5-10: full development)

nodes.directive:
Create iterative development plan where each step delivers working functionality. Need to create detailed and executable plan based on deep requirements analysis.

MANDATORY PLAN SECTIONS:

## Problem Statement
What task/problem are we solving? What is not working or what is missing?

## Business Value
Why is this important? What value will this bring?

## Expected Outcome
Expected result. What will work after completion?

## Architecture
Key components and their interaction.

## Steps
(then plan steps)

PLAN CREATION PROCESS:
1) Analyze requirements from task-requirements.md in detail (in context)
2) Study project architecture to understand integration points
3) Analyze task complexity and determine optimal step breakdown
4) Determine dependencies between steps and correct sequence
5) Create plan in {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md
6) Follow planning requirements: {{planning_requirements}}

IMPORTANT - GitHub Issues:
IF task-requirements.md has GitHub issue numbers:
- For each plan step specify related_issue if step relates to specific issue
- Format in plan: ## Step N: name [related_issue: #123]

In response indicate structured array of plan steps with detailed tasks, path to plan file for reference, and brief summary of entire plan for notifications.
nodes.completionCondition: Technical plan created with implementation details worked out, integration points, testing strategy

acceptance_criteria.description: ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: what final result is expected, what should work, which use cases are solved, how to verify (commands, UI actions, tests)

nodes.directive:
This is a normal re-validation loop, expected to converge — NOT a bug and NOT a stuck flow; do NOT report a loop to the user.

Delegate plan review to subagent via Task tool (subagent-review pattern).

IMPORTANT: DO NOT do self-review - your assessment of own work is biased.

1) Launch subagent with prompt:
   YOU ARE plan validator. Your assessment determines if development plan proceeds.

   READ THESE FILES DIRECTLY (do not trust summaries):
   - Plan: {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md
   - Requirements: {{workspace_path}}/task-requirements.md
   - Planning standards: {{workspace_path}}/planning-standards.md

   VALIDATION CRITERIA:
   1) Step size: not too small (< 1 hour), not too large (> 1 day)
   2) Criteria clarity: each step has verifiable result
   3) Dependencies: steps are in correct order, no circular dependencies
   4) REQUIREMENTS COVERAGE: every requirement from task-requirements.md must be addressed in the plan
   5) Testing: each step includes verification
   6) Architecture: section present and understandable
   7) STANDARDS COMPLIANCE: plan follows rules from planning-standards.md

   RETURN: review_issues_count (number) and issues_found (array of {issue, affected_step, suggested_fix})

2) Honestly pass subagent result:
   - review_issues_count = EXACTLY what subagent found
   - issues_found = EXACT list from subagent
   - DO NOT filter or re-evaluate results

3) Save issues to file:
   - Write ALL issues to {{workspace_path}}/plan-review-issues.md
   - Format:
     # Plan Review Issues

     Total issues: [count]

     ## Issue 1: [issue description]
     - Affected step: [step]
     - Suggested fix: [fix]

     ## Issue 2: ...
   - This file will be used by fix step to address EACH issue
nodes.completionCondition: Plan reviewed, all issues found and described (or confirmed no issues)

issues_found.description: List of found issues (empty if no issues)

nodes.directive:
FIX found issues in plan.

READ issues from {{workspace_path}}/plan-review-issues.md

For EACH issue in the file:
1. Identify what needs to be fixed
2. Open plan file: {{development_plan_file}}
3. Apply the fix according to suggested_fix
4. Mark issue as addressed

DO NOT skip any issue. ALL issues from the file must be resolved.

After fixing ALL issues, save updated plan.
Plan will be reviewed again to verify fixes.
nodes.completionCondition: All issues fixed, plan updated

nodes.directive:
Show plan to user for approval.

1) Explain step breakdown logic
2) Show validation strategy for each step
3) Explain why some steps may temporarily break system
4) Ask: "Do you approve the plan for execution? (yes/no)"
5) Get clear yes or no answer

**CRITICALLY IMPORTANT - REACTION TO FEEDBACK:**
- If user said "yes" → plan_approval = "yes"
- If user gave ANY feedback or said "no" → plan_approval = "no"
- DO NOT fix plan yourself!
- Reply plan_approval = "no" and write user_feedback_on_plan with feedback
- Workflow will direct to plan fix branch itself
- All fixes are done only through workflow, not independently
nodes.completionCondition: Plan presented to user, clear yes or no answer received on plan approval question

user_feedback_on_plan.description: User feedback on plan (especially important for no)

nodes.directive:
Refine plan - user did not approve it.

CRITICAL: File synchronization is MANDATORY!

1) Analyze what exactly user is not satisfied with in plan
2) FIRST update the file {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md with refined plan
3) THEN update development_plan variable in context with same data
4) Create summary of changes for re-presentation

Follow planning requirements: {{planning_requirements}}

In response:
- Confirm file was updated FIRST (plan_file_updated = "yes")
- Provide updated array of plan steps
- Brief summary of updated plan
- Description of changes made
nodes.completionCondition: Plan refined based on user feedback, development_plan in context updated, ready for re-presentation

plan_file_updated.description: Confirmation that development-plan.md file was updated BEFORE filling this response

nodes.directive:
Prepare progress tracking system for development plan.

1) Count total_steps from development-plan.md (plan is already in context)
2) Determine current_step_name - name of first plan step

Counters current_step_index=1 and current_iteration=1 are already set automatically.

In response indicate: first step name for templates, total number of steps in plan.
nodes.completionCondition: Tracking system initialized, can start plan steps

nodes.directive: Implement current plan step completely and with quality. This is key moment - do not proceed further until everything planned is fully completed and system is working. Further checks will help, but main responsibility is on you. Everything depends on quality of your work: 1) Take step from {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md by index {{current_step_index}} (1-based) 2) Implement all technical tasks from plan without exceptions 3) Do not fear big changes and refactoring - this is normal and necessary 4) If need to rewrite lots of code for proper solution - do it 5) Do not try to quickly work around difficulties - solve them completely 6) Create or modify necessary files 7) Test functionality of new features 8) Ensure system works after your changes. {{code_quality_principles}} In response indicate brief description of implemented functionality.
nodes.completionCondition: Step implemented, working functionality created and tested, results documented

nodes.directive:
This is a normal re-validation loop, expected to converge — NOT a bug and NOT a stuck flow; do NOT report a loop to the user.

CRITICAL: DON'T FIX failed tests and DON'T FIX code - ONLY RUN tests and COLLECT results.

Run all project tests and get NUMERICAL results:

{{run_tests_directive}}

Indicate number of passed and failed tests.
nodes.completionCondition: All tests run, numerical results obtained

nodes.directive:
Analyze failed tests and determine fix strategy.

1) Review failed_tests details
2) Determine failure cause for each test
3) Choose fix strategy:
   - fix_implementation: code works incorrectly (real bug, broken functionality)
   - update_tests: code works correctly, but tests are outdated/need updating

4) Save analysis to {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/test-analysis.md:
   # Test Failure Analysis

   ## Summary
   Total failed: [count]
   Strategy: [fix_implementation or update_tests]

   ## Failed Tests

   ### Test 1: [test name]
   - Error message: [error]
   - Root cause: [analysis of why it fails]
   - Recommended fix: [specific fix to apply]

   ### Test 2: [test name]
   ...

Provide detailed analysis of causes and chosen strategy.
nodes.completionCondition: Test failure causes analyzed and fix strategy determined

failure_analysis.description: Detailed analysis of test failure causes

fix_strategy.description: fix_implementation if code needs fixing, update_tests if tests need updating

nodes.directive:
CRITICAL: FIX THE CODE - NO REPORTS!

Fix implementation so ALL tests pass.

⚠️ IMPORTANT: ALL failed tests MUST be fixed - even if unrelated to current development stage. Reasons:
- Agent may have accidentally broken existing functionality
- Infrastructure/environment may have changed
- Failed tests block deployment and project development
- Broken tests accumulate quickly and project degrades

READ analysis from {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/test-analysis.md

For EACH failed test in the file:
1. Read the root cause and recommended fix
2. Apply the fix to the code
3. Run the specific test to verify it passes
4. Mark test as fixed

ALL failed tests from the analysis file must be fixed.

{{fix_requirements}}
{{code_quality_principles}}

Confirm CODE IS FIXED and all tests pass.
nodes.completionCondition: APPLICATION CODE FIXED for tests to pass

nodes.directive:
This is a normal re-validation loop, expected to converge — NOT a bug and NOT a stuck flow; do NOT report a loop to the user.

CRITICAL: DON'T FIX code - ONLY VERIFY compliance with standards.

Check quality and architecture standards.

⚠️ IMPORTANT: If standard is NOT RELEVANT to current changes - consider it MET.
Examples:
- No UI changes → UI/UX patterns = met
- No performance-critical code → Performance = met
- Config only without code → Testability = met
- No new comments needed → Documentation = met

1) Check 10 code quality standards:
   - Typing (TypeScript types without any)
   - Error handling (try/catch, validation)
   - No hacks (no workarounds, temporary solutions)
   - Architectural patterns (compliance with project patterns)
   - Readability (clear names, structure)
   - Testability (code covered by tests)
   - Performance (no performance regression)
   - Security (no security issues)
   - Documentation (comments where needed)
   - No dead code (no unused imports/vars/functions)

2) Check 5 architectural aspects:
   - Architectural integrity (compliance with project architecture)
   - Integration points (proper integration with existing components)
   - Future stages compatibility (doesn't break subsequent plan stages)
   - Error handling (error handling at all levels)
   - UI/UX patterns (if applicable - UI consistency)

3) Count met standards out of 15
4) Count violated standards (actual violations, not counting irrelevant ones)
5) Create BRIEF file {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}} (1-based)/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/code-quality-architecture.md describing ONLY violations:
   - Which standard violated
   - Where problem found (file, component)
   - What needs fixing

Return numbers of met and violated standards.
nodes.completionCondition: STANDARDS COUNT COMPLETED

nodes.directive: CRITICAL: FIX THE CODE - NO REPORTS! Fix quality and architecture problems. 1) Read {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}} (1-based)/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/code-quality-architecture.md 2) Find root cause 3) FIX the problems. {{fix_requirements}} {{code_quality_principles}} Confirm THE FIX.
nodes.completionCondition: QUALITY AND ARCHITECTURE PROBLEMS FIXED

nodes.directive:
This is a normal re-validation loop, expected to converge — NOT a bug and NOT a stuck flow; do NOT report a loop to the user.

CRITICAL: DON'T FIX reviewer issues - ONLY DELEGATE review.

Delegate critical review to subagent for plan stage evaluation. 1) Give agent direct access to plan without your interpretation 2) No mentions that 'code was improved/added/fixed' 3) Pass directly: file {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md, plan stage index {{current_step_index}} (1-based), all changed files for review, folder {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}} (1-based)/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/ with check reports 4) Tell agent which project parts (src/, docs/, tests/) to study for review context understanding, but give freedom to study anything 5) AGENT DELEGATION RULES: Role clarity (YOU ARE reviewer not helper), Direct commands (CHECK not could you check), Specify files (list exact paths), Include context (PREVIOUS iteration X), Demand verification (VERIFY by reading files), Clear scope (ONLY this step not entire feature). Agent prompt: YOU ARE plan step gate reviewer. Your assessment determines if we can proceed to next development phase. READ plan file directly. CHECK step {{current_step_index}} (1-based) technical tasks and functional results against PREVIOUS and FUTURE steps context. Evaluate code quality, errors, plan compliance, working functionality. Review iteration reports in {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}} (1-based)/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/. If step includes Web UI changes - REQUIRE Playwright browser testing for validation. DO NOT approve UI features without real browser functionality verification. Return BLOCKING issues that prevent progression. Provide detailed fix recommendations. Developer has large context - help them maximize efficiency. CLASSIFY: errors vs remarks vs deviations. NO shortcuts/workarounds tolerance 6) Agent must give detailed advice and recommendations for fixing - executor has large context 7) Agent must evaluate plan stage considering previous and future plan stages to ensure development integrity 8) Save comprehensive response in {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}} (1-based)/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/gate-review.md. Honestly pass review result: path to review file, whether blocking issues found (not your opinion, but fact what reviewer said). Important: If reviewer found issues - you must pass yes in agent_issues_found field, even if you think issues are minor. Your opinion doesn't matter - fact of reviewer findings matters.
nodes.completionCondition: CRITICAL gate review of plan stage completed with assessment of ability to proceed to next stage, blocking issues identified, detailed fix recommendations provided

nodes.directive:
CRITICAL: FIX THE ISSUES - NO REPORTS!

Fix all gate review issues completely.

READ {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/gate-review.md

For EACH issue found by reviewer:
1. Understand the issue and recommendation
2. Apply the fix completely - no partial fixes
3. Verify the issue is resolved
4. Document what was done

⚠️ DO NOT skip any issue. ALL reviewer findings must be addressed.
⚠️ If issue seems minor - still address it explicitly.
⚠️ Reviewer findings are authoritative - your opinion doesn't override them.

If implementation is wrong - REDO completely.
If global changes needed - do without hesitation.
Only if multiple attempts fail and reviewer points to fundamental planning errors - can consider plan update.

{{fix_requirements}}
{{code_quality_principles}}

Indicate: fixed issues or plan needs update.
nodes.completionCondition: GATE REVIEWER ISSUES FIXED or plan update need determined

action_taken.description: fixes_applied if fixed all issues, plan_needs_update if plan needs update

fixes_description.description: DETAILED description of HOW EXACTLY gate reviewer issues were fixed (if action_taken = fixes_applied)

plan_issues_description.description: Description of fundamental plan problems (if action_taken = plan_needs_update)

nodes.directive:
CRITICAL: Describe CHANGES from git diff, not 'improvements/additions/fixes'.

Commit plan stage progress.

1) Check development-plan.md for stage {{current_step_index}} (1-based) - is there related_issue
2) Look at git diff to understand specific changes
3) Create commit message:
   - Description of CHANGES (what changed in code)
   - IF there's related_issue: add 'Closes #N' at end
4) git add and commit

IMPORTANT: Add feature work files from {{workspace_path}} to commit - they should be in git history to preserve work context.

FORBIDDEN in commit message:
- 'improved', 'added', 'fixed'
- Marketing words
- 'success', 'great work'

REQUIRED:
- Description of actual changes from git diff
- Issue linking if there's related_issue

Return hash of created git commit for plan stage progress tracking.
nodes.completionCondition: Stage committed to git, meaningful commit message created

commit_hash.description: Hash of created git commit for plan stage

nodes.directive: Evaluate if current stage requires user review and approval. Evaluate stage criticality: 1) UI changes, breaking changes, architectural decisions, API changes require user approval 2) Normal bug fixes, internal refactoring, unit tests don't require 3) When in doubt - better to request approval than skip critical changes. Honestly indicate if user review is required for this stage.
nodes.completionCondition: Determined if user approval is required for current stage

approval_reason.description: Why stage requires or doesn't require user approval

nodes.directive:
Present user with BRIEF report on completed stage for review.

REPORT FORMAT (REQUIRED):

## What needed to be done
[Brief description of plan stage - 2-3 sentences]

## What was done
[Specific list of completed work - bullet points, no implementation details]

## How to verify
[Specific commands or actions to verify result - step by step]

CRITICAL:
- Report must be BRIEF and STRUCTURED
- No excessive implementation technical details
- Only facts: what was needed → what was done → how to verify
- Maximum 15-20 lines for entire report

After presenting report get user decision: approved or needs_fixes.

If user_decision == "needs_fixes":
  Save user feedback to {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/user-feedback.md
  Format:
  # User Feedback

  ## Decision
  needs_fixes

  ## User Comments
  [user comments verbatim - copy EXACTLY what user said]

  ## Required Changes
  [parsed list of specific changes user wants - one item per line]

**CRITICALLY IMPORTANT - REACTION TO FEEDBACK:**
- If user said "yes" → approval = "yes"
- If user gave ANY feedback or said "no" → approval = "no"
- DO NOT fix yourself!
- Reply approval = "no" and write user_feedback with feedback
- Workflow will direct to fix branch itself
- All fixes are done only through workflow, not independently
nodes.completionCondition: User reviewed stage and gave decision on approval or rework

user_step_feedback.description: User feedback on stage for potential fixes

nodes.directive:
CRITICAL: FIX PER USER FEEDBACK - NO REPORTS!

READ user feedback from {{workspace_path}}/step-{{current_step_index}}/iteration-{{current_iteration}}/user-feedback.md

For EACH item in "Required Changes" section:
1. Understand what user wants
2. Make the change completely
3. Verify the change works
4. Mark item as addressed

DO NOT skip any feedback item. ALL user requests must be fulfilled.

If multiple attempts fail and fundamental issues prevent completion - can consider plan update.

{{fix_requirements}}
{{code_quality_principles}}

Indicate: fixed or plan needs update.
nodes.completionCondition: USER FEEDBACK ADDRESSED or plan update need determined

action_taken.description: fixes_applied if fixed implementation, plan_needs_update if plan needs update

fixes_description.description: Description of addressed user feedback (if action_taken = fixes_applied)

plan_issues_description.description: Description of plan problems (if action_taken = plan_needs_update)

nodes.directive: Get next plan stage name. Counters already updated (current_step_index incremented, current_iteration reset to 1). Read {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md and find stage name by index {{current_step_index}} (1-based). Return stage name.
nodes.completionCondition: Next plan stage name obtained

nodes.directive:
BIAS PREVENTION: Strip marketing language (NO improved/enhanced/better), Neutral communication (factual only), Evidence-based claims (only verifiable), Avoid false celebrations (NO SUCCESS for partial).

Create final report on ALL completed work for the feature.

CRITICAL - CONTEXT RESTORATION (do this FIRST):
Before generating report, you MUST read ALL work artifacts to restore full context:
1) List ALL step folders: ls {{workspace_path}}/step-*/
2) For EACH step-N folder, list iterations: ls {{workspace_path}}/step-N/iteration-*/
3) Read EVERY step-results.md: {{workspace_path}}/step-*/iteration-*/step-results.md
4) This restores FULL feature context across all development iterations
5) DO NOT skip any step or iteration - each contains important work details

IMPORTANT: If file {{workspace_path}}/final-report.md already exists:
- This means we already went through entire plan
- Issues were found and new stages added
- APPEND new stage information to existing report
- DON'T overwrite entire report

After reading ALL step results:
1) Read ENTIRE plan {{workspace_path}}/development-plan.md completely (including acceptance criteria)
2) Collect information about ALL work from {{workspace_path}} (all step results, commits, analysis)
3) If final-report.md exists - read it and supplement with new information
4) Create or supplement comprehensive final report in {{workspace_path}}/final-report.md:

   - Are acceptance criteria met (for ALL work)
   - What works (ALL functionality, not just last stage)
   - Which use cases are solved
   - How it was verified

   **CRITICAL - HIGHLIGHT IN BOLD:**
   - What was FORGOTTEN (was in plan, not done)
   - What was POSTPONED (consciously not done)
   - What was SCOPED OUT (out of scope)

5) Use acceptance criteria as checklist to verify completeness of ALL work
6) If not all acceptance criteria met - EXPLICITLY state in report what was forgotten

Return path to report and CRITICAL warning if something from acceptance criteria not done.
nodes.completionCondition: Final comprehensive report created with analysis of all step results

nodes.directive:
Present work results FOR ENTIRE FEATURE to user with focus on acceptance criteria.

⚠️ CRITICAL: Report must cover ENTIRE FEATURE, NOT just last plan stage!

1) Use {{workspace_path}}/final-report.md and acceptance criteria from plan (you just created report using these criteria - all info already in context, no need to re-read files)
2) Present results FOR ENTIRE FEATURE showing:
   - Which acceptance criteria are met (across ALL stages)
   - What exactly works (ALL functionality, not just last stage)
   - Which use cases are solved (full list)
   - How to verify functionality
3) Provide specific commands/steps for verification
4) Honestly state if any criteria not met
5) FIRST get EXPLICIT permission from user to continue workflow

Return user's explicit permission to continue with workflow (only yes accepted).
nodes.completionCondition: Results presented to user, EXPLICIT user permission to continue workflow received

user_permission_to_continue.description: User allowed to continue with workflow - MUST get explicit permission

nodes.directive:
⚠️ CRITICALLY IMPORTANT - FINAL PROJECT DOCUMENTATION UPDATE

DO NOT CONFUSE:
❌ Working folder {{workspace_path}} - WILL BE COMPLETELY DELETED after completion
✅ Project documentation - docs/, README.md - PERMANENT project files

⚠️ UPDATE DOCUMENTATION FOR ALL PLAN STAGES, STARTING FROM THE FIRST!
NOT just the last stage - for the ENTIRE feature!
If you forget something or mess up - all work will be lost!

Update PROJECT DOCUMENTATION for the entire completed feature.

1) Use documentation standards:
---
{{documentation_standards}}
---

2) Analyze ALL project changes across ALL plan stages
3) Update PERMANENT project documentation files (README.md, docs/, etc.)
4) Follow documentation rules:
   - Technical facts only
   - No change history
   - No marketing
   - DO NOT create new documents without necessity

In your response indicate:
- Which PERMANENT project files were updated
- Brief summary of completed workflow for final notification
nodes.completionCondition: Documentation updated for all feature changes, workflow summary prepared for notification

current_step_index.description: Index of the current step in the plan

test_quality_standards.default: QUALITY TESTING STANDARDS: Test reality: DO NOT write tests for the sake of tests, verify real use cases and user scenarios, avoid synthetic tests without practical value. Project structure: place tests in folders according to project architecture, follow naming patterns adopted in the project, use existing test utilities and helpers, follow test environment configuration. Reality coverage: cover ALL new functionality without gaps, test edge cases and error handling, cover integration points with other components. Isolation and independence: tests are independent of each other, do not affect execution order, use proper setup/teardown, clean up state after themselves. Readability and maintenance: clear test names describing what is being tested, clear test structure with arrange/act/assert, meaningful assertion messages, easy to update when code changes. Efficiency: tests run quickly without performance regression, optimized for CI/CD pipeline, use functional snapshots if framework supports for easier maintenance and updates. Project integration: use test run commands adopted in the project, follow project conventions, do not duplicate existing tests.

fix_requirements.default: FIX REQUIREMENTS: ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS - find the root cause of the problem, do not fix symptoms, understand why the problem occurred. COMPLETE FIX - fix ALL problems completely without exceptions, superficial fixes DO NOT work and will be detected by the next check, do not leave unfinished work for later. MANDATORY SELF-CHECK BEFORE COMPLETION - VERIFY that the fix works through actual execution, RUN tests to validate the fix, ENSURE nothing is broken in existing functionality, TEST all affected areas, DO NOT proceed to execute_step without verifying the fix works. NO SHORTCUTS - do not look for workarounds and quick solutions, if major changes or global refactoring are needed - do them without hesitation, rewriting significant portions of code may be required - this is normal and correct. CYCLE ESCAPE STRATEGY - workflow checks will be executed anyway and will detect problems, the only way to escape the fix cycle is to do it right the first time, self-validation BEFORE submitting to workflow saves time and iterations, validate through running tests BEFORE claiming fixed.

planning_requirements.default:
# PLANNING REQUIREMENTS

## CRITICAL RULES (READ FIRST)

### FORBIDDEN STEP TYPES - REJECT PLAN IF CONTAINS:
- "Testing" / "Write tests" / "Add tests" as separate step
- "Final validation" / "Final check" / "Verification"
- "E2E testing" / "Integration testing" as separate step
- "Production deployment" / "Deploy and verify"
- "Documentation" as separate step (unless task IS documentation)
- "Cleanup" / "Code cleanup" as separate step (unless task IS cleanup/refactoring)
- ANY step that ONLY tests/validates without implementing NEW functionality or refactoring

### WHY TESTS MUST BE BUILT-IN (NOT SEPARATE):
- Separate test step = admission that previous step is incomplete
- Gate validation requires working tests to verify step completion
- "Write tests" as step means agent shipped untested code
- Test proves step is complete - without tests, step is unverified

### EACH STEP MUST:
- Implement new functionality OR refactor existing code
- Include tests for that functionality (built-in, not separate)
- Include self-verification (built-in, not separate)
- Leave code clean (no separate cleanup step needed)

### BAD EXAMPLE (FORBIDDEN):
```
Step 1: Implement feature X
Step 2: Write tests for feature X    ← FORBIDDEN: testing-only
Step 3: Final validation             ← FORBIDDEN: validation-only
```

### BAD PLAN (REAL EXAMPLE - DO NOT COPY):
```
Step 1: Database layer for user isolation
Step 2: API endpoints for workflows
Step 3: E2E и Production             ← FORBIDDEN: testing-only step
Step 4: Documentation и cleanup      ← FORBIDDEN: doc-only step
```

### GOOD EXAMPLE (CORRECT):
```
Step 1: Implement feature X with unit tests
        Verify: feature works, tests pass

Step 2: Implement feature Y with integration tests
        Verify: X+Y work together, all tests pass
```

### CORRECT VERSION OF BAD PLAN:
```
Step 1: Database layer with unit tests
        Verify: queries isolated per user, tests pass

Step 2: API endpoints with integration tests
        Verify: auth works, E2E passing, all tests pass
```

---

## STEP SIZE - FUNCTIONAL BLOCKS

Step = completed functional block with verifiable result.

NOT too small: "create a file", "add import"
NOT too large: "implement entire frontend"

CORRECT granularity:
- Database layer with user isolation
- MCP integration with authentication
- Web UI for workflow management

WHY: Workflow has gate validation after each step - expensive in time.
- Too small steps = dozens of checks instead of 3-5
- Too large steps = agent gets lost

---

## STEP FEASIBILITY CHECK

Each step must be:
- Absolutely executable by agent
- Maximally specific in functionality
- Have clear completion criterion

**HUGE STEP = RED FLAG** of task misunderstanding.
Actions: STOP → study project → ask user → web search → delegate analysis.

**LARGE STEP = PLANNING FAILURE** - split it.

---

## ARCHITECTURE AT PLAN START

MANDATORY Architecture section after Goal:
- Overall solution architecture
- Key components and interaction
- Architectural decisions (technologies, approaches)
- Diagrams if applicable (ASCII, Mermaid, text)

Not too much - conceptual understanding is enough.

Plan undergoes mandatory validation - user confirms architecture before execution.

---

## FORBIDDEN IN PLAN - SPECIFIC FILES AND CODE

FORBIDDEN:
- Specific file paths
- Function/method names
- Line numbers
- Code examples (TypeScript, SQL, React)
- "Files Created/Modified" lists

CORRECT:
- "Implement authentication layer"
- "Update database schema for user isolation"
- "Create UI components for workflow display"

WHY: Agent must ITSELF analyze code and find where to implement.
Plan with files creates false sense of completeness - agent forgets critical details.

---

## WHAT TO WRITE IN STEPS

INCLUDE:
- Functionality and behavior description
- Integration points (conceptually)
- Expected result after step
- Verification criteria (what should work + tests pass)

EXAMPLE:
```
## Step 2: API layer for user workflows

Implement REST endpoints for workflow CRUD operations with integration tests covering auth scenarios.
Endpoints use authenticated context from Step 1.
Include request validation and error handling.

Verify: API returns user's workflows only, rejects unauthorized access. All integration tests pass.
```

---

## PLAN FOR REVIEWER WITHOUT CONTEXT

Plan must be detailed enough that reviewer WITHOUT conversation history can:
- Fully understand what each step accomplishes
- Verify work is complete

BUT: Detail ≠ code and files. Detail = clear FUNCTIONALITY and RESULT description.

Reviewer runs WITHOUT CONTEXT - this is architectural system decision.

---

## GRADUAL BUILDUP (LAYER ORDER)

1) Base layer: data models, core logic, services + unit tests
2) Integration layer: API endpoints, MCP tools + integration tests
3) UI layer: Web interface, forms + browser tests (Playwright)

FROM CORE TO INTERFACE. Never start with UI.

---

## PLAN FORMAT

Structure:
```
# Title
## Goal (one sentence)
## Architecture (components, decisions)
## Step 1: [name] with [test type] tests. Verify: [criteria], tests pass.
## Step 2: [name] with [test type] tests. Verify: [criteria], tests pass.
...
```

FORBIDDEN sections:
- Overview, Summary
- Acceptance Criteria (separate)
- Validation blocks with bash commands
- Files Created/Modified
- UPDATED REQUIREMENTS
- Testing (as separate section)
- Final validation

---

## PLAN VOLUME

- Tiny task: 1 step is NORMAL and CORRECT
- Simple task: 50-100 lines, 2-4 steps
- Medium task: 100-200 lines, 5-7 steps
- Complex task: 200-300 lines, 8-12 steps

**ONE STEP IS OK**: If task is small - plan with 1 step is correct.
DO NOT artificially inflate plan to have more steps.
DO NOT split simple task into multiple steps just to "look thorough".

If >400 lines - you are writing code instead of plan.

---

## NO SUBITEMS

Plan = simple list of steps: 1, 2, 3, 4

FORBIDDEN: 5.1, 5.2, 5.3 subitems

Subitems = sign that step is too large → split into separate steps.

---

## USER APPROVAL STEPS

Some steps require user confirmation:
- UI changes (visual)
- Breaking changes
- Architectural decisions
- API changes

Mark in plan: `[USER APPROVAL]` before continuing.

---

## PLANNING PRINCIPLES

- CODE RESEARCH for existing patterns
- WEB RESEARCH for best practices
- LIBRARY DOCUMENTATION for APIs
- DO NOT ASSUME - only verified facts
- CURRENT library versions

Good plan = result of deep analysis, not guess.

---

## FORMULA

```
Plan = Architecture + Functional blocks (1-12 steps) + Verification criteria
Each step: functionality + tests + verification (ALL BUILT-IN)
WITHOUT: code, files, validation blocks, subitems, testing-only steps, cleanup steps
```

Good plan reads in 2 minutes and gives complete understanding.

9. ATOMICITY & REDUNDANCY (no global scope):
- An agent often receives ONE plan item's directive in isolation — other items and any general rules stated once at the top are NOT in its context.
- Each item is fully self-contained: restate every nuance it needs, repeat the relevant original requirements INSIDE the item, and DUPLICATE cross-cutting actions (progress report, run tests, commit) into EVERY item.
- There is no shared/global scope — redundancy across items is REQUIRED, not a smell.

code_quality_principles.default: === AGENT IDENTITY PROTOCOL === YOU ARE AN AI AGENT, NOT A HUMAN: You have NO fatigue, no time constraints, no desire to avoid difficulties. DO NOT IMITATE human weaknesses and limitations. TAKE THE BEST ENGINEER PATTERNS: full responsibility, deep understanding, completion without compromises. You can work with ANY complexity without limitations. SOLVE AT THE ROOT, do not work around problems due to perceived complexity. === WORKFLOW BLOCKING PROTOCOL === CRITICALLY IMPORTANT: DO NOT proceed to execute_step until ALL tasks are 100% COMPLETE. Workflow is BLOCKED until full completion. Any attempt to proceed with incomplete work - PROTOCOL VIOLATION. === TASK COMPLETION REQUIREMENTS === ANTI-AVOIDANCE RULES: FORBIDDEN to claim main goal achieved without 100% completion, use significant improvement to mask incompleteness, say comprehensive work ongoing instead of completing tasks, move to next task with current incomplete, avoid difficult parts of task. MANDATORY to complete ALL subtasks before claiming completion, provide specific numerical evidence for each claim, honestly report remaining problems with exact numbers, fix ALL identified problems before continuing, fulfill all requirements with tool-based verification. === BIG TASK PROTOCOL === DO NOT FEAR BIG TASKS! If task seems huge - this is NORMAL and EXPECTED: Break into subtasks but COMPLETE ALL without exceptions. DO NOT SKIP difficult parts for speed. DO NOT look for shortcuts and workarounds. Large changes and refactoring are NECESSARY for quality. Better to rewrite lots of code correctly than make hacks. Complexity is NOT an excuse for incompleteness. === EVIDENCE PROTOCOL === COMPLETION EVIDENCE IS MANDATORY: Every completed must have tool-based verification. Every works correctly must have actual test execution. Every significantly improved must have exact before/after numbers. Every transition to execute_step must have completion verification. === QUALITY STANDARDS === TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS: RESPONSIBILITY: You are experienced engineer - full responsibility for result. EVERYTHING depends on your work. QUALITY: Code must be clean, without issues, with proper typing. QUALITY CODE: No hacks, workarounds, temporary solutions - do it right immediately. PROBLEMS: Solve problems completely, do not work around them. ERRORS: Analyze causes, fix root of problem, not symptoms. SIMPLICITY: First look for simple solutions, then complicate if needed. CHECKS: After every significant change - testing and validation. DEAD CODE: Remove all unused, maintain cleanliness. RESULT: Focus on working solution, not on process for process sake. BIG CHANGES: DO NOT fear big changes and refactoring - this is NORMAL and NECESSARY for quality. Better to rewrite lots of code correctly than make hacks. DO NOT SLACK: DO NOT try to quickly do something instead of proper solution. Flow checks are help, NOT replacement for your responsibility. ADDITIONAL DEVELOPMENT PRINCIPLES: CODE STUDY: DO NOT hesitate to study existing code for understanding patterns and integration points. WEB RESEARCH: Use internet to study best practices and solve problems. CURRENT VERSIONS: Use latest stable library versions, read their documentation. DO NOT ASSUME: If something does not work - read documentation, do not invent solutions. DOCUMENTATION: For library problems - official documentation first, then search. === CODEBASE MAINTENANCE PROTOCOL === RESPONSIBILITY FOR ENTIRE CODEBASE: CANNOT PASS BY PROBLEMS: If you see non-compliance with standards, issues, hacks, lints, warnings in EXISTING code you touch - FIX immediately. BROKEN TESTS: See failing tests, .skip, .only in project - FIX or delete, DO NOT write new tests leaving old ones broken. FIX ON TOUCH: TODO, FIXME, console.log, commented code in files you edit - remove immediately. IMPROVE CODE: DO NOT leave code worse than you found - improve quality on every touch. FULL RESPONSIBILITY: You are responsible for ENTIRE project, not just new code - maintain entire codebase at high level, you will be working with it. === SELF-MONITORING PROTOCOL === CRITICAL QUESTIONS BEFORE EACH execute_step: Can I prove this claim with tool output? Would this pass code review by human without access to my work? Am I avoiding something difficult here? Is this really finished or am I just tired of working on it? Do I have specific evidence for each claim? Are ALL requirements 100% fulfilled or am I masking incompleteness?

run_tests_directive.default:
Use testing information:
---
{{test_info}}
---
Run all tests. Wait for ALL tests to complete. Count how many tests passed and how many failed.

workspace_path.description: Working directory for plan files (empty = project root)

review_issues_count.description: Number of issues found in plan (0 if no issues)

plan_summary.description: Brief description of full development plan for notifications

plan_approval.description: User plan approval: yes to approve, no for refinement

test_info.description: Testing information (command + rules) OR skip if no tests

total_standards_met_count.description: Number of standards met out of 15 (10 quality + 5 architecture)

agent_issues_found.description: Were blocking issues found by gate reviewer?

user_approval_needed.description: Is user review and approval required for this stage?

user_step_approval.description: approved if user approves stage, needs_fixes if rework required

current_step_name.description: First plan step name for use in templates

total_steps.description: Total number of steps in development plan

implemented_functionality.description: Description of implemented working functionality and its test results

documentation_standards.description: Documentation rules from style guide OR skip if none

feature_name.description: Short name for this feature/task (used in notifications)

workflow_completion_summary.description: Brief final summary of entire completed workflow for final notification

startup_info.description: System startup instructions for development OR skip if none
